raw_case:
Cardiac angiosarcoma with lung metastasis presented with multiple halo signs: One case report and literature review
Cardiac angiosarcoma is a rare aggressive malignancy with rapid progress and poor prognosis. Here we report 1 patient with cardiac angiosarcoma with lung metastasis, which presented as multiple halo signs and ground glass opacities. The patient underwent computed tomography (CT) guided lung biopsy and postoperative tissue histology confirmed the diagnosis of angiosarcoma. Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) and cardiac Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) identified the main tumor in the right atrium. Positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) excluded intraabdominal lesions. The patient was given chemotherapy with nab-paclitaxel, cardiac radiation therapy and remained follow-up. Considering the rapid disease progression and poor prognosis, the present case report is intended to provide diagnostic insight into cardiac angiosarcoma with lung metastasis, especially with lung CT scans of multiple halo signs and ground glass opacities.

Introduction

Angiosarcoma is a soft tissue sarcoma that originates from vascular or lymphatic endothelial cells. Cardiac angiosarcoma is the most common primary cardiac neoplasm, which accounts for approximately 25%-30% of all primary cardiac malignancy [1]. It predominantly affects adults aged 40-50 years and most occur in the right atrium. Here, we present a case of a 41-year-old man diagnosed with primary cardiac angiosarcoma with lung metastasis presented with multiple halo signs and ground glass opacities.

Case report

A 41-year-old smoking male driver presented with cough, exertional dyspnea, intermittent hemoptysis for 4 months. There was no fever, headache, weight loss, skin eruption, or chest pain. He visited several hospitals and underwent brain CT scan, PET/CT, gastroscopy and colonoscopy, which showed no obvious findings. TTE showed pericardial and pleural effusion. Cytological examination from pericardial and pleural drainage showed proliferative mesothelial cells. Physical examination revealed a body temperature of 36.8 °C, heart rate of 110 beats per minute (bpm), blood pressure of 123/84 mmHg, oxygen saturation of 95 %. Electrocardiography (ECG) showed a 2: 1 block atrial flutter with a heart rate of 140 bpm. Considering his presentations and frequent exposure to cigarette, as well as the multiple halo signs and ground glass opacities radiographic findings (Fig. 1A), infections like virus, mucorcosis, atypical or hypersensitivity pneumonitis was suspected initially. However, there was no improvement after empirical antimicrobial and corticosteroid treatments. Autoantibodies (including Extractable Nuclear Antigen (ENA) Antibodies Panel, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, Antiglomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibody) were all negative. Diagnostic bronchoscopy were performed, which showed multiple intrabronchial hemorrhage (Fig. 2). Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF) cellularity and transbronchial lung biopsy (TBLB) tissue pathology were nonspecific. Initial cultures (blood, sputum, and lavage fluid) were negative. BALF targeted next generation sequencing (tNGS) test indicated aspergillus flavus, debaryomy ces hansenii, human beta herpesvirus 7 and human rhinovirus, which did not corelate with clinical menifestations. CT guided lung biopsy was then performed and postoperative tissue histological examination revealed poorly differentiated spindle-shaped cells. Immunohistochemical staining were positive for cluster of differentiation 31 (CD31), CD34, friend leukemia integration 1(Fli-1), ERG, Vimentin, P53, integrase interactor 1 (INI-1), but negative for cytokeratin (CK), thyroid transcription factor 1 (TTF1), Napsin-A, Cam5.2, epithelial membrane antigen (EMA), and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA). Semiquantitative estimation of Antigen Kiel-67 (Ki-67) index was 40% (Figs. 3A-E). TTE and MRI (Fig. 1B) identified the main tumor in the right atrium. PET/CT excluded intra-abdominal lesions. High grade and stage IV right atrial angiosarcoma with pulmonary metastases was diagnosed. The patient was given chemotherapy with nab-paclitaxel, cardiac radiation therapy and remained follow-up.

Discussion

Angiosarcoma is a soft tissue sarcoma that originates from vascular or lymphatic endothelial cells. They are defined “high-grade” because of their aggressive behavior [2]. Angiosarcoma can occur in any in any part of the body and more commonly in older patients with cutaneous form. Primary cardiac angiosarcoma is the most common primary cardiac neoplasm, which accounts for approximately 25%-30% of all primary cardiac malignancy [1]. Because they are highly aggressive, most cases have already developed distant metastasis at the time of diagnosis. Metastasis are common, including lung, liver, lymph nodes, adrenal gland, bone, and brain, in which lungs being the most frequently affected site.Symptoms of cardiac angiosarcoma are often nonspecific, which depend on local invasion and systemic metastasis, such as atrioventricular block arrhythmias, cough, hemoptysis, fever, dyspnea, and abdominal pain, making early diagnosis challenging. In the present case, the the patient was admitted to the hospital with dyspnea, occasional cough and hemoptysis, and was already with lung metastasis when confirming the diagnosis. TEE has sensitivity of 75% identifying the cardiac mass. Cardiac MRI with contrast is helpful characterizing the tumor and its vascularization [3]. PET/CT is useful for potential metastasis evaluation. Nontheless, the golden standard for the diagnosis must be based on histopathological and immunohistochemical results.Halo sign of lung CT scan is defined as parenchymal lung consolidation or nodules surrounded by ground-glass opacity attenuation, indicating alveolar hemorrhage surrounding by pulmonary infarction. Although halo sign was first described in immunosuppressed patients with Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis (IPA), other infectious conditions like mucormycosis, Cryptococcus, candida, respiratory syncytial virus, cytomegalovirus, and noninfectious conditions like lymphomatoid granulomatosis, sarcoidosis, primary lung neoplasm, lung metastases like angiosarcoma, melanoma, lymphoma should also be considered [4].The histological features of angiosarcoma are characterized by pleomorphic, malignant endothelial cells. Immunohistochemical staining are positive for CD31, Fli-1, von Willebrand factor, vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), and Ulex europaeus agglutinin 1. Other markers like CD34, cytokeratin and vimentin can also be useful. Currently the treatment is very challenging because of rapid progress and poor prognosis, especially if there is metastatic stage. Surgical resection with involved margins is the primary treatment if there is local confined disease. For advanced or metastatic stage diseases, chemotherapy with anthracyclines, taxanes or ifosfamide is the first line of treatment. Adjuvant radiotherapy should also be considered. Angiosarcoma originates from vascular endothelium, however, anti-VEGF therapy with bevacizumab failed to gain benefit in a phase II study [5]. Genomic sequencing study showed facial and scalp angiosarcomas have high tumor mutation burden and ultraviolet light mutational signature that may have potential responsiveness to checkpoint inhibitors [6]. Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) are effective in some case series [7], however, in a retrospective study, patients with advanced cardiac angiosarcoma failed the ICIs treatment because of tumor progression [8]. Treatment with ICIs still need to be tested in a prospective trial for further study. For this case, we are undergoing chemotherapy and radiotherapy and follow up to see if there is durable responses.

Conclusion

From this case report, we could learn that early diagnosis is critical for the optimal treatment of cardiac malignancies. Lung metastasis with angiosarcoma should be noticed if there are halo signs, especially in patients with immunocompetent conditions.

Patient consent

Written informed consent was obtained from patient and his family for publication.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 41-year-old male, smoker, occupation as a driver.  
- **Chief Complaint:** Cough, exertional dyspnea, and intermittent hemoptysis for 4 months.  
- **History of Present Illness:** Symptoms persisted for 4 months; no fever, headache, weight loss, rash, or chest pain. No response to antimicrobial or corticosteroid treatments for suspected infections.  
- **Past Medical History:** None provided.  
- **Personal History:** Smoker; occupation involves probable environmental exposures.  
- **Family History:** None provided.  
- **Physical Examination:** Temperature 36.8°C, HR 110 bpm, BP 123/84 mmHg, SpO2 95%; no other significant findings.  
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - **Initial Investigations:**  
    - Imaging: Lung CT showed halo signs and ground-glass opacities.  
    - TTE: Pericardial and pleural effusion; significant findings include 2:1 atrial flutter with HR 140 bpm.  
    - Cytology: Analysis of pericardial and pleural fluid revealed only proliferative mesothelial cells.  
    - Bronchoscopy: Multiple intrabronchial hemorrhages; BALF cellularity nonspecific; cultures and infectious markers negative.  
    - Autoantibodies: Negative autoimmune markers (e.g., ANCA, ENA panel).  
  - **Advanced Diagnostic Tests:**  
    - Next-generation sequencing of BALF: Indicated non-clinically corroborative findings (e.g., aspergillus, rhinovirus).  
    - CT-guided lung biopsy: Poorly differentiated spindle-shaped cells; immunohistochemical markers positive for CD31, CD34, Fli-1, Vimentin, ERG, P53, INI-1; negative for epithelial markers. Ki-67 proliferation index: 40%.  
    - Cardiac Imaging (TTE, MRI): Identified a tumor in the right atrium (cardiac mass).  
    - PET/CT: Excluded intra-abdominal metastasis.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Suspicions:** Based on the patient's pulmonary findings (halo signs, ground-glass opacities) and history of smoking, initial differential diagnoses included:  
   - Infectious conditions (e.g., viral infections, mucormycosis, or aspergillosis). However, no clinical improvement with antibiotics or corticosteroids, negative cultures, and lack of clear infectious etiology excluded these.  
   - Hypersensitivity pneumonitis: Absence of relevant exposures and specific findings made this less likely.  
   - Autoimmune causes (e.g., vasculitis, Goodpasture's syndrome): Negative autoimmunity markers excluded these.  

2. **Consideration of Neoplastic Causes:** Upon exclusion of infectious and autoimmune etiologies, the diagnostic focus shifted to malignancies. The lung biopsy and immunohistochemical markers revealed endothelial origin (CD31, CD34 positivity) consistent with angiosarcoma.  

3. **Localizing the Primary Tumor and Staging:**  
   - Cardiac imaging identified a right atrial mass, strongly suggesting primary cardiac angiosarcoma.  
   - PET/CT findings and histology confirmed metastatic lung involvement without intra-abdominal lesions.  

4. **Other considerations excluded:** Secondary metastatic diseases (e.g., melanoma, lymphoma) were excluded based on pathology and negative markers (e.g., CK, TTF1, CEA).
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Primary cardiac angiosarcoma with lung metastasis (stage IV). This diagnosis was based on histological evidence (poorly differentiated spindle-shaped endothelial cells), right atrial tumor on imaging, and pulmonary metastatic pattern presenting as halo signs. The immunohistochemical profile and Ki-67 index supported the aggressive high-grade malignancy.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Primary cardiac angiosarcoma with pulmonary metastasis.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let me start by going through the case details carefully. The patient is a 41-year-old male smoker with a job as a driver, which might involve exposure to environmental pollutants. His main complaints are cough, exertional dyspnea, and intermittent hemoptysis over four months. No fever, weight loss, or chest pain. He didn't respond to antimicrobials or corticosteroids, so infections or typical inflammatory conditions might be less likely.

First, considering the symptoms: hemoptysis can be from various causes like infections (TB, fungal), cancer, pulmonary embolism, or vasculitis. The lack of response to antimicrobials suggests something non-infectious. The CT findings of halo signs and ground-glass opacities are non-specific but can be seen in conditions like vasculitis (e.g., GPA), malignancies, or alveolar hemorrhage.

The TTE showed pericardial and pleural effusion, along with atrial flutter. The presence of effusions could be due to malignancy, infections, autoimmune diseases, or heart failure. The cytology of the fluids showed proliferative mesothelial cells but no malignant cells, which might suggest reactive effusions rather than metastatic cancer, but not definitive.

Bronchoscopy found intrabronchial hemorrhages. BALF was nonspecific, and cultures were negative, ruling out common infections. Autoantibodies were negative, making autoimmune diseases like GPA or lupus less likely, though not impossible if seronegative.

Advanced tests: Next-gen sequencing found aspergillus and rhinovirus, but it's stated as non-clinically corroborative. So maybe contaminants or not the primary issue. CT-guided lung biopsy is key here. The biopsy shows poorly differentiated spindle cells. The immunohistochemistry markers CD31, CD34, Fli-1, ERG, Vimentin are all positive. These markers are typically associated with endothelial differentiation. CD31 and CD34 are markers for vascular tumors. Fli-1 and ERG are transcription factors involved in endothelial and hematopoietic cells. Vimentin is a mesenchymal marker. The Ki-67 of 40% suggests a high proliferation rate, indicating malignancy. Negative epithelial markers rule out carcinoma.

Then, cardiac imaging found a tumor in the right atrium. Combining the lung biopsy results with the cardiac mass, the primary tumor might be in the heart with metastasis to the lungs. Alternatively, a primary lung tumor with cardiac involvement, but the markers point towards a vascular tumor.

The key here is the immunohistochemistry. CD31, CD34, and ERG positivity are indicative of vascular tumors. Spindle cell morphology with these markers suggests a sarcoma of endothelial origin. Angiosarcoma is a possibility. Angiosarcomas can arise in the heart, although rare, and often present with metastases. The presence of pericardial and pleural effusions might be due to the tumor's location in the right atrium causing obstruction or direct invasion.

PET/CT excluded abdominal metastasis, so the primary is likely thoracic. Putting it all together: a high-grade malignant vascular tumor (angiosarcoma) originating in the right atrium, with lung metastases. The hemoptysis and pulmonary lesions would be metastatic disease. The spindle cells and immunohistochemical profile align with angiosarcoma. Also, the occupational exposure as a driver isn't directly linked, but smoking is a general risk factor, though angiosarcoma's etiology isn't clear.

Other possibilities could include other sarcomas, but the markers strongly suggest endothelial origin. Epithelioid hemangioendothelioma is another vascular tumor but usually less aggressive. The high Ki-67 and poor differentiation point towards angiosarcoma.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s symptoms (cough, hemoptysis, exertional dyspnea) and imaging findings (halo signs, ground-glass opacities) initially suggest pulmonary pathology. The lack of response to antimicrobials/corticosteroids and negative infectious/inflammatory markers (autoantibodies, cultures) argue against infection or autoimmune disease.  
<step 2> Lung biopsy reveals poorly differentiated spindle cells with immunohistochemical markers (CD31, CD34, Fli-1, ERG) indicative of endothelial differentiation. These markers, along with vimentin positivity and high Ki-67 (40%), strongly support a malignant vascular tumor.  
<step 3> Cardiac imaging identifies a right atrial mass, which, combined with the lung biopsy findings, suggests a primary cardiac tumor with pulmonary metastasis. The absence of epithelial markers (e.g., cytokeratins) excludes carcinoma.  
<step 4> The immunohistochemical profile (CD31, CD34, ERG) and spindle-cell morphology align with angiosarcoma, a high-grade endothelial malignancy. Cardiac angiosarcoma often metastasizes to the lungs, explaining the pulmonary lesions, hemorrhages, and effusions.  
<step 5> PET/CT excluding distant metastasis beyond the thorax supports localized spread from the cardiac primary. Occupational/environmental exposures and smoking are non-specific but may contribute to genetic susceptibility.  

### Answer:

reasoning_eval.step: The patient’s symptoms (cough, hemoptysis, exertional dyspnea) and imaging findings (halo signs, ground-glass opacities) initially suggest pulmonary pathology. The lack of response to antimicrobials/corticosteroids and negative infectious/inflammatory markers (autoantibodies, cultures) argue against infection or autoimmune disease.

Lung biopsy reveals poorly differentiated spindle cells with immunohistochemical markers (CD31, CD34, Fli-1, ERG) indicative of endothelial differentiation. These markers, along with vimentin positivity and high Ki-67 (40%), strongly support a malignant vascular tumor.

Cardiac imaging identifies a right atrial mass, which, combined with the lung biopsy findings, suggests a primary cardiac tumor with pulmonary metastasis. The absence of epithelial markers (e.g., cytokeratins) excludes carcinoma.

The immunohistochemical profile (CD31, CD34, ERG) and spindle-cell morphology align with angiosarcoma, a high-grade endothelial malignancy. Cardiac angiosarcoma often metastasizes to the lungs, explaining the pulmonary lesions, hemorrhages, and effusions.

PET/CT excluding distant metastasis beyond the thorax supports localized spread from the cardiac primary. Occupational/environmental exposures and smoking are non-specific but may contribute to genetic susceptibility.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Initial suspicions based on pulmonary findings (halo signs, ground-glass opacities) and smoking history include infectious conditions (viral infections, mucormycosis, aspergillosis), hypersensitivity pneumonitis, and autoimmune causes (vasculitis, Goodpasture's syndrome). Exclusion criteria: no clinical improvement with antibiotics/corticosteroids, negative cultures, absence of relevant exposures, specific findings, and negative autoimmunity markers.

<Step 2> Diagnostic focus shifted to malignancies after excluding infectious and autoimmune etiologies. Lung biopsy and immunohistochemical markers showed endothelial origin (CD31, CD34 positivity) consistent with angiosarcoma.

<Step 3> Cardiac imaging identified a right atrial mass, strongly suggesting primary cardiac angiosarcoma. PET/CT findings and histology confirmed metastatic lung involvement without intra-abdominal lesions.
<Step 4> Secondary metastatic diseases (melanoma, lymphoma) were excluded based on pathology and negative markers (CK, TTF1, CEA).

<Step 5> Final diagnosis: Primary cardiac angiosarcoma with lung metastasis (stage IV), based on poorly differentiated spindle-shaped endothelial cells, right atrial tumor on imaging, pulmonary metastatic pattern (halo signs), immunohistochemical profile, and Ki-67 index supporting an aggressive high-grade malignancy.
